theory.canonical_statement: The Trawin base unit is the invariant measure obtained by integrating the metric density around one fundamental loop that encircles the zero point exactly once. It translates the singular geometry into a reproducible unit scale rather than borrowing measurement from an external convention.
theory.technical_interpretation: The Trawin base unit $\TrawinBase$ is defined by the invariant relation u\_T:=$\int$\Gamma$∣g∣ dℓu\textbackslash\_\{\text\{T\}\}:=\int\_\{\Gamma\}\sqrt\{\textbar g\textbar\}\textbackslash,\text\{d\}\ellu\_T:=$\int$\Gamma$∣g∣dℓ taken over a fundamental loop $\Gamma$ encircling the TZP exactly once.\\\

equation_progression.interpretive_role: This turns the singular geometry into a concrete scalar that can function as the framework's internal measurement unit.
This prevents overcounting by restricting the unit to the simplest nontrivial loop around the anchor.
This closes the progression by making the unit independent of a raw path description and dependent only on its admissible TRAWIN class.

external_sources.title: Trawin Base Unit
external_sources.canonical_claim: The Trawin base unit is the invariant measure obtained by integrating the metric density around one fundamental loop that encircles the zero point exactly once. It translates the singular geometry into a reproducible unit scale rather than borrowing measurement from an external convention.
external_sources.exact_language: This source (Hamilton's Least Action Principle and Noether's Theorem) supports the physical law (variational principle) by showing that The lecture explains that Hamilton established that dynamical equations can be derived using the calculus of variations to minimize an integral of the Lagrangian along a path.
external_sources.remaining_gap: While the cited source provides partial support, a dedicated mathematical or physical proof specific to the Trawinistic framework is still required to fully establish this entry.
external_sources.source_title: Hamilton's Least Action Principle and Noether's Theorem
external_sources.relevance: The lecture explains that Hamilton established that dynamical equations can be derived using the calculus of variations to minimize an integral of the Lagrangian along a path. This integral is called the action, and the system follows the path of least action. This supports the idea that a base unit can be defined by integrating a metric density around a fundamental loop, analogous to evaluating an action integral.

promotion_ledger.note: Append-only proof-support promotion; final physical certificate still requires review of cited source fit.
